docs: On February 24, 2022, Russia garnered international attention by initiating an invasion of Ukraine, thereby instigating a protracted and intense conflict that persists to the present day. President Vladimir Putin of Russia has articulated various public rationales for the incursion. This analysis will examine the justifications provided by Putin, their correspondence with established facts, and other plausible motivations for Russia's military intervention in a sovereign and independent state. Historically, Ukraine was a constituent republic of the Soviet Union before asserting its independence, a status solidified through a referendum conducted shortly before the dissolution of the USSR in December 1991. The nation has upheld its autonomy since that time. Nevertheless, Putin continues to characterize Ukraine as an integral part of Russia, challenging its status as a distinct nation. Notably, in a conversation with then-US President George W. Bush in 2008, Putin asserted that Ukraine did not qualify as a sovereign state. Stephen Hall, a scholar specializing in Russian politics at the University of Bath in the UK, indicates that a significant segment of the Russian populace shares this perspective, suggesting that it extends beyond merely the Kremlin's stance. Hall posits that Russia perceives Kyiv, the capital of Ukraine, as the "mother of Russian cities," leading to the contention that Putin cannot tolerate its existence outside Russian sovereignty. Furthermore, Hall argues that Russia's assertion over Ukraine is essential to substantiate its claims of being a historical great power, which purports a lineage extending back millennia. He contends that without Ukraine, "Russia cannot claim a thousand years of history because Kyiv was already in existence 1,200 years ago, when Moscow was a forest."

In July 2021, Vladimir Putin published an essay entitled "On the Historical Unity of Russians and Ukrainians," in which he asserted that Russians and Ukrainians constitute "one people." In the context of the impending invasion, Putin contended that Ukraine was established by Russian Bolsheviks and argued that it "never had a tradition of genuine statehood." American historian Timothy Snyder characterized Putin's assertions as a form of imperialism, while British journalist Edward Lucas referred to it as historical revisionism. Additionally, various observers have noted that the Russian leadership maintains a skewed perception of contemporary Ukraine and its historical narrative, with these misrepresentations being reinforced and disseminated through state policy. In March and April 2021, Russia initiated a significant military build-up along the Russo-Ukrainian border, which was followed by a second build-up from October 2021 to February 2022, encompassing both Russia and Belarus. Members of the Russian government consistently denied any intentions to invade or attack Ukraine, including statements from government spokesman Dmitry Peskov on 28 November 2021, Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Ryabkov on 19 January 2022, Russian ambassador to the United States Anatoly Antonov on 20 February 2022, and Russian ambassador to the Czech Republic Alexander Zmeevsky on 23 February 2022.

In early 2021, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy implemented measures against pro-Russian oligarchs, notably targeting Viktor Medvedchuk, a close associate of President Vladimir Putin. In response, Putin increased troop deployments near the Ukrainian border and published an article asserting that Russians and Ukrainians constitute “one people.” By December of the same year, tens of thousands of Russian troops had amassed at the borders, accompanied by Putin's demands directed at NATO and the United States, including a request that Ukraine be permanently excluded from NATO, a proposition that the Biden administration rejected.

In 2014, the Ukrainian regions of Donetsk and Luhansk seceded from Ukraine, a move that the Ukrainian government attributed to the influence of what it deemed Russian-backed insurgents. Following a deterioration of relations with NATO and Western nations in late February, Putin formally recognized these territories as independent states and deployed military forces to ostensibly “maintain peace.” Shortly after this recognition, Russia initiated a comprehensive invasion of Ukraine, commencing in the eastern region of Donbas. In response, Zelenskyy declared martial law and severed diplomatic relations with Russia. Putin's actions drew widespread condemnation both globally and domestically within Russia. Following months of Russian military advances, Ukrainian forces successfully repelled the Russian military, recovering over a thousand square miles of territory.

The Northeastern Ukraine campaign, Eastern Ukraine campaign, Southern Ukraine campaign, and the 2023 Ukrainian counteroffensive, along with other regional military operations, naval actions, spillover and cross-border incidents, resistance efforts, and related attacks on civilians, are critical components of the ongoing conflict. On 24 February 2022, the Russian Federation launched an invasion of Ukraine, marking a significant escalation in the Russo-Ukrainian War that commenced in 2014. This invasion has resulted in the deaths of tens of thousands of individuals on both sides. Russian military actions have been associated with widespread civilian casualties and allegations of torture against captured Ukrainian personnel. By June 2022, approximately 8 million Ukrainians had been internally displaced, while over 8.2 million had sought refuge outside the country by May 2023, resulting in the most extensive refugee crisis in Europe since World War II. The conflict has also led to substantial environmental degradation, often characterized as ecocide, which has contributed to global food crises. Prior to the initiation of hostilities, Russian military forces amassed near the borders of Ukraine, despite official denials of any intention to undertake military action. President Vladimir Putin declared a "special military operation" aimed at supporting the self-proclaimed republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, whose armed forces were engaged in conflict with Ukraine in the Donbas region. He asserted that the operation's objectives were to "demilitarise" and "denazify" Ukraine, while also promoting irredentist perspectives that questioned Ukraine's sovereignty and inaccurately depicting its governance as that of neo-Nazis persecuting the ethnic Russian population.

He inaccurately asserted that they had been "facing humiliation and genocide perpetrated by the Kyiv regime." Furthermore, Putin incorrectly alleged that Ukraine's government comprised neo-Nazis under Western influence, that Ukraine was in the process of developing nuclear weapons, and that NATO was augmenting military infrastructure in Ukraine with the intention of threatening Russia. He articulated that Russia aimed for the "demilitarisation and denazification" of Ukraine. Putin maintained that he had no intentions of occupying Ukraine and expressed support for the right of the Ukrainian populace to self-determination. Shortly after Putin's declaration, Russian missiles targeted various locations across Ukraine, and Russian military forces initiated an invasion from the north, east, and south. Subsequently, a purported report from Russia's Federal Security Service (FSB) was disclosed, indicating that the intelligence agency had been unaware of Putin's invasion plans. The invasion commenced at dawn on 24 February, characterized by the deployment of infantry divisions along with armored and air support in Eastern Ukraine, accompanied by numerous missile strikes throughout the country, reaching as far west as Lviv. The initial skirmishes occurred in Luhansk Oblast, near the village of Milove on the border with Russia, at 3:40 a.m. Kyiv time.
